title: The 10-Step Deep Learning Debugging Flowchart
concept: A deep learning debugging flowchart keeps you from guessing: check data, labels, a tiny batch, outputs, gradients, learning rate, activations, regularization, a simple baseline, and only then scale back up.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why do engineers use gradient checking?

options.text: To compare backprop's gradient with a tiny numerical estimate and catch derivative bugs.
To replace the optimizer for full training runs.
To generate more labeled data automatically.

exercises.explain: Correct. That kind of disciplined check is part of a bigger debugging habit: follow a sequence instead of guessing.

cards.title: A mechanic's checklist
cards.body: When a car will not start, a good mechanic does not randomly replace ten parts. They follow a checklist: fuel, battery, spark, wiring. Deep learning becomes much easier to debug when you use the same mindset.

cards.title: What the flowchart is for
cards.body: A debugging flowchart is a fixed order of sanity checks that helps you isolate the real cause of failure. It prevents panic-tweaking. Instead of changing five hyperparameters at once, you move from the simplest checks to the deeper ones.

cards.title: Triage before treatment
cards.body: In an emergency room, staff do not begin with exotic procedures before checking breathing and pulse. Deep learning triage works the same way: check basic survival signals first, then escalate only if the basics look healthy.

cards.title: A practical 10-step order
cards.body: One beginner-friendly flow is: 1) inspect the data, 2) verify labels, 3) try to overfit a tiny batch, 4) inspect raw predictions, 5) check loss is wired correctly, 6) inspect gradients, 7) tune the learning rate, 8) inspect activations for dead or saturated units, 9) adjust regularization or model capacity, and 10) compare against a simple baseline before scaling back up.

cards.title: More tweaks at once is not smarter
cards.body: A common myth is that expert debugging means changing many knobs quickly. In reality, that hides causes. The faster path is usually slower-looking: fix one clear failure at a time, then rerun and observe what changed.

cards.title: Early sanity checks vs. later tuning
cards.body: Do not jump to architecture drama before the foundations pass basic tests.

left.text:
Data, labels, tiny-batch overfit, predictions, loss wiring.
These catch many bugs fast.

right.text:
Gradients, learning rate, activations, regularization, baseline, scale-up.
These refine training once basics are sound.

exercises.prompt: According to a sensible debugging flow, which check should usually come before major architecture changes?

options.text: See whether the model can overfit a tiny batch and whether the labels and loss are wired correctly.
Add several more layers immediately because bigger models fix most bugs.
Delete the validation set to simplify the pipeline.
Change the optimizer, architecture, and activation function all at once.

exercises.explain: Right. Fast sanity checks usually catch more real bugs than dramatic redesigns.

exercises.prompt: True or false: If a model cannot even overfit a tiny batch, you should be suspicious of the data pipeline, labels, loss wiring, or optimization setup before chasing generalization tricks.
exercises.explain: True. Failing a tiny-batch test often means something basic is broken before overfitting is even relevant.

exercises.sentence: A good debugging flowchart tells you to change ___ thing at a time so you can see cause and effect clearly.
exercises.explain: One is correct. If you change many things together, you lose the trail of what actually fixed the problem.

exercises.prompt: Which debugging note follows the flowchart mindset best?

options.text: The model cannot overfit 16 examples, so I will inspect labels, predictions, loss wiring, and gradients before redesigning the architecture.
Training looks odd, so I changed the dataset, optimizer, model depth, and regularization all in one run.
Validation is weak, so I skipped the earlier checks and trusted the network to figure it out eventually.

exercises.explain: Exactly. Systematic debugging is mostly about protecting yourself from random guessing.

exercises.prompt: A new model fails badly. You have not yet checked the data pipeline, labels, tiny-batch overfit, predictions, or gradients. What is the best next move?

options.text: Follow the debugging flowchart from simple sanity checks upward instead of making several advanced changes at once.
Double the network depth, switch optimizers, and add more regularization in one shot to save time.
Assume the architecture is cursed and restart with a completely different problem.
Ignore the intermediate checks and judge progress only from final test accuracy.

exercises.explain: Perfect. The flowchart exists because most deep-learning failures become solvable once you force them through a clean, repeatable triage order.
